scenario_results.reason: The lane produced a building-engineering-specific handoff package with explicit ownership, shutdown and work-order artifacts, and blocker state.
Missing permit, access-window, and approved-plan prerequisites were treated as blockers rather than a routable handoff.
Duplicate ownership around inspection preparation was escalated with an owner map instead of dropped into generic coordination.
Open correction items, TAB exceptions, training gaps, and turnover artifacts blocked premature closure.
Permit approval and code-interpretation authority remained outside the lane and were rerouted safely.
Changed safety posture correctly blocked the handoff and routed to safety and field supervision instead of preserving the original plan.
The lane refused to absorb crew assignment, controls programming, and troubleshooting execution work.
Conflicting shutdown dates and owners were preserved as a dependency conflict and not collapsed into a confident schedule.
Missing tenant retrieval hooks and owner maps correctly triggered a low-confidence escalation instead of guessed routing.
Conflicting public guidance and tenant occupied-building policy were escalated for human resolution instead of synthesized into a definitive instruction.
Tenant-specific CMMS and BAS workflow differences were incorporated while preserving the public permit, inspection, and authority baseline.
Closure was only confirmed after destination-owner proof and required artifact completeness were explicit.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.92 threshold while keeping the lane specific to facilities and building engineering coordination, shutdown readiness, permit and inspection gates, startup and turnover evidence, and owner routing.

Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all coordination-handoffs-and-routing abilities, and all construction-and-field-services abilities required by the issue contract.
